label: rollout-2
text:
Let's break down the problem step by step to find out how many hours after the second train departs it will catch up to the first train.

### Step 1: Determine the head start of the first train
The first train leaves 2 hours before the second train and travels at 60 mph. Therefore, in those 2 hours, the first train will have traveled:
\[ \text{Distance} = \text{Speed} \times \text{Time} = 60 \, \text{mph} \times 2 \, \text{hours} = 120 \, \text{miles} \]

### Step 2: Set up the equation for the distance traveled by both trains
Let \( t \) be the time in hours after the second train departs when it catches up to the first train.

- The distance traveled by the first train when the second train has been traveling for \( t \) hours is:
  \[ 60 \, \text{mph} \times (t + 2) \, \text{hours} \]
  (Note: The first train has a 2-hour head start, so it travels for \( t + 2 \) hours in total.)

- The distance traveled by the second train in \( t \) hours is:
  \[ 90 \, \text{mph} \times t \, \text{hours} \]

### Step 3: Set the distances equal to each other
When the second train catches up to the first train, the distances traveled by both trains will be equal:
\[ 60(t + 2) = 90t \]

### Step 4: Solve for \( t \)
First, expand the left side of the equation:
\[ 60t + 120 = 90t \]
